I kind of can=E2=80=99t believe this worked? Here are Bloomberg=E2=80=99s P=
hilip Lagerkranser and Joanna Ossinger this morning:

Bitcoin and other cryptocurrencies suddenly tumbled on Tuesday in Asia, dri=
ven by questions surrounding the balance sheet of Alameda Research, billion=
aire Sam Bankman Fried=E2=80=99s crypto trading house. Investors kept pulli=
ng assets from FTX, the exchange Bankman-Fried also runs, and sent its nati=
ve token FTT diving as much as 32%.=C2=A0
The turbulence, triggered by rival Zhao =E2=80=9CCZ=E2=80=9D Changpeng=E2=
=80=99s decision to sell a roughly $530 million holding of FTT coins, broug=
ht back memories of the contagion that ripped through crypto markets after =
the TerraUSD stablecoin collapsed in early May. That upheaval then gave way=
 to months of unusual stability in cryptocurrencies =E2=80=94 a lull that a=
ppeared to have ended this week.=C2=A0
Bankman-Fried, whose investments in troubled crypto firms in June led some =
to compare him to the legendary banker John Pierpont Morgan, has found hims=
elf trying to assuage investors, customers and counterparties. On Monday, h=
e tweeted that =E2=80=9Cunfounded rumors=E2=80=9D had been circulating and =
that FTX keeps audited financials.=C2=A0
=E2=80=9CThere are market players who smell blood and are adding to the dow=
nward price pressure,=E2=80=9D said Cici Lu, chief executive of blockchain =
consulting firm Venn Link Partners.=C2=A0

And here are=C2=A0Bloomberg=E2=80=99s Yueqi Yang and Olga Kharif   a bit la=
ter in the morning:

A 48-hour crypto drama ended in shock Tuesday as Binance Holdings Ltd. agre=
ed to acquire its most formidable rival, FTX.com, after helping whip up an =
investor exodus from billionaire Sam Bankman Fried=E2=80=99s three-year-old=
 exchange.
The sharp turn of events will reshape the more than $1 trillion industry am=
id a possibly prolonged market downturn. The two founders made the announce=
ment on Twitter concurrently. =E2=80=9CTo protect users, we signed a non-bi=
nding LOI, intending to fully acquire FTX.com and help cover the liquidity =
crunch,=E2=80=9D Changpeng Zhao, CEO of Binance, said in a tweet. Terms wer=
en=E2=80=99t disclosed.
=E2=80=9CA huge thank you to CZ, Binance, and all of our supporters,=E2=80=
=9D said Bankman-Fried, CEO of FTX.com, on Twitter. =E2=80=9COur teams are =
working on clearing out the withdrawal backlog as is. This will clear out l=
iquidity crunches; all assets will be covered 1:1. This is one of the main =
reasons we=E2=80=99ve asked Binance to come in.=E2=80=9D

There you go!? Here is  Zhao=E2=80=99s Twitter thread on the deal, and here=
 is=C2=A0 Bankman-Fried=E2=80=99s. =E2=80=9CThis afternoon, FTX asked for o=
ur help. There is a significant liquidity crunch,=E2=80=9D begins CZ, who e=
mphasized that the deal is a letter of intent subject to due diligence and =
that =E2=80=9CBinance has the discretion to pull out from the deal at any t=
ime.=E2=80=9D SBF emphasized that =E2=80=9Cthe important thing is that cust=
omers are protected.=E2=80=9D
This is all pretty late-breaking and it is a little hard to tell what is go=
ing on, but one can make some assumptions. My main assumption is that if yo=
u are a crypto exchange facing a =E2=80=9Csignificant liquidity crunch,=E2=
=80=9D and you call up a bigger crypto exchange to ask for help, and you si=
gn a deal for them to buy you the same day, then the price that they are pa=
ying you is, roughly speaking, zero. I mean! Probably it is not actually ze=
ro. But the basic levers here are:
=09If you are a crypto exchange and you have a liquidity crunch and you can=
not process withdrawals, and you=C2=A0don=E2=80=99t=C2=A0get a bailout, the=
n your future equity value is basically zero. If you tweet to all your cust=
omers =E2=80=9Csorry we=E2=80=99re out of money for a bit so no more tradin=
g and we=E2=80=99re going to hang on to=C2=A0your=C2=A0money, but things wi=
ll get better,=E2=80=9D they will not get better.=C2=A0=09You know that, an=
d the buyer knows that, so if they say =E2=80=9Cwe will buy your exchange, =
make sure that all your customers are made whole, and give you a Snickers b=
ar in exchange for 100% of the equity,=E2=80=9D that is better than you=E2=
=80=99ll do=C2=A0without=C2=A0them.=09You don=E2=80=99t have a ton of time =
to negotiate, and they don=E2=80=99t have a ton of time to do due diligence=
. How does the buyer know that there are no huge disasters lurking on your =
balance sheet? They don=E2=80=99t, and they don=E2=80=99t have much time to=
 find out, and your liquidity crunch is not an encouraging=C2=A0sign. You a=
re having a disaster now!=09Obviously if=C2=A0lots=C2=A0of buyers are compe=
ting to buy you then you can do=C2=A0better than a Snickers bar, if solving=
 your liquidity crunch will fix the problem and you are a profitable exchan=
ge with valuable assets. But if you are a very big exchange there are not t=
hat many buyers, and if you need a deal=C2=A0today=C2=A0then your chances o=
f spinning up an auction are low.

Is FTX  worth tens of billions of dollars, if it can get through this week =
of heavy withdrawals and negative press? Maybe! Will Binance buying it allo=
w it to get through this week of heavy withdrawals and negative press and r=
eturn to profitability? Probably! Is Binance paying FTX tens of billions of=
 dollars for its equity? I would be very, very, very=C2=A0surprised!
There is precedent. In my world, the most famous precedent is probably JPMo=
rgan Chase & Co.  buying Bear Stearns Cos. for $2 per share one Sunday in 2=
008, =E2=80=9Cless than one-tenth the firm=E2=80=99s market price on Friday=
.=E2=80=9D (Later the price was revised up.) If you need a bailout from JPM=
organ over the weekend, JPMorgan will step in and make sure that your busin=
ess can keep operating and that your customers will get paid and that the f=
inancial system does not collapse, but=C2=A0you=C2=A0won=E2=80=99t get paid=
 much.
But in the crypto world, the famous precedents are pretty much Sam Bankman-=
Fried   bailing out=C2=A0crypto lenders this summer. FTX   bailed out Block=
Fi Inc., getting an option to buy it for  as little as $15 million or as mu=
ch as $240 million; it had been valued at $3 billion in 2021. Alameda helpe=
d=C2=A0out Voyager Digital with a $75 million loan, and FTX ultimately=C2=
=A0agreed to  buy its assets out of bankruptcy, cashing out customers but p=
aying something like $51 million for its actual business; its equity market=
 capitalization was more than $1 billion in April. This summer large crypto=
 firms were on sale at pennies on the dollar if you had some ready cash and=
 a tolerance for risk; Bankman-Fried did. (As it happens, he=C2=A0 explaine=
d his process to me=C2=A0at the Bloomberg Crypto Summit in July. You can wa=
tch on YouTube; we get into it at around the 9:30 mark.) Now, it seems, his=
 large crypto firm was on sale at pennies on the dollar, and Zhao has the c=
ash.
One question that might be=C2=A0too early to answer is: Why was there a liq=
uidity crunch in the first place? A crypto exchange is a weird sort of busi=
ness, in many ways more like a=C2=A0brokerage=C2=A0than a traditional excha=
nge. The simplest way to run the business is to take deposits from customer=
s, buy crypto for the customers, keep everything segregated, and make money=
 on commissions. Coinbase Global Inc.=E2=80=99s balance sheets are public, =
and pretty simple: It has about $101 billion of customer cash and crypto as=
sets, and about $101 billion of offsetting customer liabilities. If the cus=
tomers asked for their $101 billion back, presumably Coinbase would just gi=
ve it to them.
  [1]

A more complicated way to do it is to provide leverage to customers: Instea=
d of taking $100 of customer cash to buy $100 worth of Bitcoin, you take $1=
00 of customer cash to buy $200 worth of Bitcoin. A lot of FTX=E2=80=99s bu=
siness is in perpetual futures, a leveraged product, sometimes levered 20 t=
o 1. If you are an exchange and you are in this sort of=C2=A0business, you =
will need to come up with the extra $100 to lend to your customer. Presumab=
ly that doesn=E2=80=99t come from your equity: You are doing some sort of b=
orrowing, perhaps from other customers,
  [2]
=C2=A0perhaps from outside=C2=A0financing sources, perhaps from your affili=
ated hedge fund, etc. You will have some customers who owe you money, and o=
thers whom you owe money. You will be like a bank. If everyone to whom you =
owe money demands their money back at once, you will need to get the money =
back from the ones who owe you money, which might be hard. (You might not h=
ave a contractual right to demand the money back right away, or it might be=
 rude and bad for business, or you might have to liquidate them to get the =
money back and that would blow up the value of your collateral.) In broad s=
trokes this is a reasonable description of what happened to Bear Stearns, a=
 brokerage that financed its customers=E2=80=99 positions. If you are a cry=
pto exchange that provides leverage, then you are probably bank-like enough=
 for a run on the bank.
Another complicated way to do it is that you take your customers=E2=80=99 a=
ssets and go invest them in whatever sounds good to you. You=E2=80=99re hol=
ding Solana tokens for a customer, you invest them in some yield-farming th=
ing to make some extra cash, if the customer asks for the tokens back you d=
on=E2=80=99t have them, etc. This is more or less what brought down the Cel=
siuses and Voyagers and BlockFis of the world, but I=C2=A0would not have ex=
pected it from FTX. =E2=80=9CFTX has enough to cover all client holdings,=
=E2=80=9D Bankman-Fried tweeted yesterday.=C2=A0=E2=80=9CWe don=E2=80=99t i=
nvest client assets (even in treasuries).=E2=80=9D=C2=A0
We   talked about this situation yesterday, back when=C2=A0Bankman-Fried  w=
as tweeting =E2=80=9CA=C2=A0competitor is trying to go after us with false =
rumors. FTX is fine.=C2=A0Assets are fine.=E2=80=9D I wrote:

If you are the CEO of a big crypto firm [and] there are negative rumors abo=
ut a big competitor, do you take to Twitter to say =E2=80=9Cwe=E2=80=99re n=
ot a scam like those guys=E2=80=9D? One advantage of doing that is that you=
 might win some business and harm a competitor; crypto is a smaller busines=
s than it was a year ago, so winning market share is very valuable. Another=
 advantage is that you don=E2=80=99t want people thinking that you=E2=80=99=
re a scam; saying =E2=80=9Cwe=E2=80=99re not a scam like those guys=E2=80=
=9D is one way to persuade people that you aren=E2=80=99t.=C2=A0
The disadvantage is that it is probably in your long-term interest for peop=
le to be confident that=C2=A0none=C2=A0of the big crypto businesses are sca=
ms. =E2=80=9CCrypto is full of scams and you can=E2=80=99t trust even the b=
iggest exchanges=E2=80=9D is a=C2=A0bad message for a crypto exchange to se=
nd; adding =E2=80=9Cexcept us, you can trust us=E2=80=9D=C2=A0does not help=
.

Well. Another advantage of doing it is that a day later you might be able t=
o buy your valuable competitor for peanuts.=C2=A0One possible reading of th=
is situation is that CZ did some tweets and got himself a $30 billion crypt=
o exchange for pennies on the dollar. But it seems like a dangerous game to=
 play. Bankman-Fried was rescuing crypto firms a few months ago; now he nee=
ds a rescue. What lessons should Zhao learn from that?

   =20
      Robinhood IPO Access
   =20
 =20

We have   talked a   couple of   times before about Robinhood Markets Inc.=
=E2=80=99s =E2=80=9CIPO Access=E2=80=9D program, where Robinhood customers =
got to buy some initial public offerings at the IPO price. The pitch to Rob=
inhood customers, I have argued, is that the customers get to buy stock at =
the IPO price and thus benefit from the =E2=80=9CIPO pop=E2=80=9D when the =
stock starts trading. Instead of watching institutions buy at $100 in the I=
PO and then having to buy at $150 in the aftermarket, you can buy at $100 i=
n the IPO. The pitch to IPO issuers, I have argued, is that if Robinhood cu=
stomers buy in the IPO they will pump up the IPO price, so that the=C2=A0is=
suer=C2=A0can benefit from the IPO pop: Instead of selling to institutions =
at $100 and watching the stock run to $150 in the aftermarket as Robinhood =
customers buy it, the company can just sell directly to the Robinhood custo=
mers at $150. These pitches cannot both be entirely true.
That is oversimplified and perhaps unfair, but if you believe it then you w=
ould expect the Robinhood IPOs to perform poorly: The shares should be over=
priced in the IPO, and then trade worse afterward=C2=A0than other, non-Robi=
nhood IPOs. The product that Robinhood was marketing to issuers was, in som=
e sense,=C2=A0price-insensitive demand. You=E2=80=99d expect its customers =
to get a worse price.
Anyway here is =E2=80=9CRobinhood Gave Its Customers Access to IPOs That Al=
l Flopped=E2=80=9D:

A Bloomberg analysis of the program, IPO Access, suggests it has been a flo=
p for investors so far.=C2=A0
The initiative, which allowed users to buy shares of buzzy companies like S=
weetgreen Inc. and Allbirds Inc. before they debuted on stock exchanges, re=
sulted in losses for customers who still hold the shares. In an already dis=
mal market for IPOs, those offered by Robinhood have performed even worse.
All 23 IPOs that Robinhood opened up to its customers have declined by doub=
le-digit percentages since the stocks debuted. Five -- Iris Energy Ltd., So=
no Group NV, Vaxxinity Inc., Stronghold Digital Mining Inc. and Argo Blockc=
hain Plc -- lost at least 89% of their value.

Well, yes, right.

      Don=E2=80=99t call bribes chickens
   =20
 =20

That is a headline that   I have used before, because there is some history=
 of people using =E2=80=9Cchicken=E2=80=9D as a weird euphemism/codename fo=
r =E2=80=9Cbribe.=E2=80=9D But here is  the more literal version:

A new video released by a Chinese anti-corruption agency revealed a corrupt=
 Chinese official=E2=80=99s unusual method of hiding his ill-gotten gains.
Last April, authorities in the eastern province of Zhejiang launched an off=
icial probe into Jiang Xunbo, a retired top official at the prefecture-leve=
l city of Quzhou, who was accused of receiving millions in bribes from diff=
erent corporate executives. Jiang confessed a day after the investigation b=
egan.=C2=A0
The video, posted on Sunday by the Zhejiang provincial supervisory committe=
e on the social media platform WeChat, showed footage of officers raiding h=
is mansion. They found bags after bags of frozen meat in his freezer, inclu=
ding chickens, ducks, and fish stuffed with plastic-wrapped gold bars and j=
ewelery.

      Have your weekly staff meeting on the big screen
   =20
 =20

The   strategy of AMC Entertainment Holdings Inc. for the last year or two =
has   pretty much   been =E2=80=9Cannounce meme stuff,=E2=80=9D and while I=
 am not sure that still works the way it used to, I do admire  their commit=
ment to the bit:

AMC Theatres=C2=A0(NYSE: AMC and APE
  [3]
), the largest theatrical exhibitor in the United States and the world, and=
 Zoom Video Communications, Inc. (NASDAQ:ZM) today announced an innovative =
partnership that will turn some AMC locations throughout the United States =
into Zoom Rooms.
As hybrid work has become more commonplace throughout the United States, Zo=
om Rooms at AMC will enable companies and other entities with decentralized=
 workforces and customer bases to bring people from different markets toget=
her at the same time for cohesive virtual and in-person events and meeting =
experiences. This combines the excellent experience of Zoom with the comfor=
t and state-of-the-art sight and sound technology of AMC=E2=80=99s modern a=
nd centrally located theatres.
AMC and Zoom currently anticipate launching Zoom Rooms at AMC in up to 17 m=
ajor U.S. markets sometime in 2023.
Upon launch, Zoom Rooms at AMC users will be able to easily book online, an=
d in so doing can select their preferred theatres and meeting time. They wi=
ll receive a three-hour block of time to virtually host their event across =
multiple markets at the selected theatres. AMC and Zoom will provide the ne=
cessary equipment for a fully functional Zoom Rooms experience, in the comf=
ort of multiple movie theatres in multiple cities simultaneously. Typical a=
uditorium sizes are expected to range between 75 and 150 seats, depending u=
pon the theatre.
Additional services including food and beverage offerings, possible movie v=
iewings, and concierge-style personalized handling of meeting needs will al=
so be available at an added cost.
The announcement is yet another innovative initiative launched by AMC desig=
ned to maximize the value creation that can come from its vast amount of se=
ating capacity.

Is this a plausible=C2=A0bet on the future of work? =E2=80=9CEveryone will=
=C2=A0work from home, but will want to gather in large groups for hybrid on=
line meetings with large groups of colleagues in other cities, with popcorn=
=E2=80=9D? It is not really what=C2=A0I=C2=A0want, but it is not the crazie=
st possible theory. I suppose it hedges AMC=E2=80=99s=C2=A0reliance on peop=
le doing stuff (seeing movies) in person, and Zoom=E2=80=99s reliance on pe=
ople doing stuff (work meetings) remotely. You can go to the theater in per=
son to do a remote work meeting.
Byrne Hobart  writes:
The other notable thing about this deal is that AMC was hurt by Covid on th=
e revenue side, but helped on the financing side, since it acquired an army=
 of dedicated retail investors. They may have decided that their company is=
 structurally long the pandemic and its second-order effects, and have foun=
d a way for this to affect their fundamentals as well as their stock price.
Another possibility is that the pandemic was, for quasi-mystical reasons th=
at no one quite understands, a golden age for AMC, and they are doing every=
thing they can=C2=A0to recapture that.=C2=A0If you just=C2=A0remind=C2=A0pe=
ople of the pandemic, maybe they will buy AMC stock? =E2=80=9CZoom meetings=
,=E2=80=9D you say, and that triggers a Pavlovian response where AMC=E2=80=
=99s stock goes up.=C2=A0It doesn=E2=80=99t have to make sense; it is a sor=
t of sympathetic magic, =E2=80=9Cdo pandemic things and then AMC=E2=80=99s =
stock goes up.=E2=80=9D

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This is all pretty late-breaking and it is a l=
ittle hard to tell what is going on, but one can make some assumptions. My =
main assumption is that if you are a crypto exchange facing a =E2=80=9Csign=
ificant liquidity crunch,=E2=80=9D and you call up a bigger crypto exchange=
 to ask for help, and you sign a deal for them to buy you the same day, the=
n the price that they are paying you is, roughly speaking, zero. I mean! Pr=
obably it is not actually zero. But the basic levers here are:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">If you are a crypto exchange and you =

ney back from the ones who owe you money, which might be hard. (You might n=
ot have a contractual right to demand the money back right away, or it migh=
t be rude and bad for business, or you might have to liquidate them to get =
the money back and that would blow up the value of your collateral.) In bro=
ad strokes this is a reasonable description of what happened to Bear Stearn=
s, a brokerage that financed its customers=E2=80=99 positions. If you are a=
 crypto exchange that provides leverage, then you are probably bank-like en=
ough for a run on the bank.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Another complicated way to do it is that you t=
ake your customers=E2=80=99 assets and go invest them in whatever sounds go=
od to you. You=E2=80=99re holding Solana tokens for a customer, you invest =
them in some yield-farming thing to make some extra cash, if the customer a=
sks for the tokens back you don=E2=80=99t have them, etc. This is more or l=
ess what brought down the Celsiuses and Voyagers and BlockFis of the world,=
 but I=C2=A0would not have expected it from FTX. =E2=80=9CFTX has enough to=
 cover all client holdings,=E2=80=9D <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomberg=
business.com/click/29633808.286504/aHR0cHM6Ly90d2l0dGVyLmNvbS9TQkZfRlRYL3N0=
YXR1cy8xNTg5NTk4Mjg1Nzk4NzA3MjAy/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB9faa1d53" itempro=

000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-=
bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"=
>Bankman-Fried tweeted yesterday</a>.=C2=A0=E2=80=9CWe don=E2=80=99t invest=
 client assets (even in treasuries).=E2=80=9D=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">We <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusin=
ess.com/click/29633808.286504/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL=

solid; background-color: #ccc;">  talked about this situation yesterday</a>=
, back when=C2=A0Bankman-Fried <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusine=
ss.com/click/29633808.286504/aHR0cHM6Ly90d2l0dGVyLmNvbS9TQkZfRlRYL3N0YXR1cy=
8xNTg5NTk4Mjg0MzIyMzI4NTc5/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBad58789f" itemprop=3D"S=
toryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000=
; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom=
-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> was =
tweeting</a> =E2=80=9CA=C2=A0competitor is trying to go after us with false=
 rumors. FTX is fine.=C2=A0Assets are fine.=E2=80=9D I wrote:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If you are the CEO of a big crypto firm [and] =
there are negative rumors about a big competitor, do you take to Twitter to=
 say =E2=80=9Cwe=E2=80=99re not a scam like those guys=E2=80=9D? One advant=
age of doing that is that you might win some business and harm a competitor=
; crypto is a smaller business than it was a year ago, so winning market sh=
are is very valuable. Another advantage is that you don=E2=80=99t want peop=
le thinking that <em>you=E2=80=99re</em> a scam; saying =E2=80=9Cwe=E2=80=
=99re not a scam like those guys=E2=80=9D is one way to persuade people tha=
t you aren=E2=80=99t.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The disadvantage is that it is probably in you=
r long-term interest for people to be confident that=C2=A0<em>none=C2=A0</e=
m>of the big crypto businesses are scams. =E2=80=9CCrypto is full of scams =
and you can=E2=80=99t trust even the biggest exchanges=E2=80=9D is a=C2=A0<=
em>bad message for a crypto exchange to send</em>; adding =E2=80=9Cexcept u=
s, you can trust us=E2=80=9D=C2=A0<em>does not help.</em></p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Well. Another advantage of doing it is that a =
day later you might be able to buy your valuable competitor for peanuts.=C2=
=A0One possible reading of this situation is that CZ did some tweets and go=
t himself a $30 billion crypto exchange for pennies on the dollar. But it s=
eems like a dangerous game to play. Bankman-Fried was rescuing crypto firms=
 a few months ago; now he needs a rescue. What lessons should Zhao learn fr=
om that?</p>

 issuers, I have argued, is that if Robinhood customers buy in the IPO they=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">That is oversimplified and perhaps unfair, but=

ard=C2=A0than other, non-Robinhood IPOs. The product that Robinhood was mar=
keting to issuers was, in some sense,=C2=A0<em>price-insensitive demand</em=
>. You=E2=80=99d expect its customers to get a worse price.</p>

<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A Bloomberg analysis of the program, IPO Acces=
s, suggests it has been a flop for investors so far.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The initiative, which allowed users to buy sha=
res of buzzy companies like Sweetgreen Inc. and Allbirds Inc. before they d=
ebuted on stock exchanges, resulted in losses for customers who still hold =
the shares. In an already dismal market for IPOs, those offered by Robinhoo=
d have performed even worse.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">All 23 IPOs that Robinhood opened up to its cu=
stomers have declined by double-digit percentages since the stocks debuted.=
 Five -- Iris Energy Ltd., Sono Group NV, Vaxxinity Inc., Stronghold Digita=
l Mining Inc. and Argo Blockchain Plc -- lost at least 89% of their value.<=
/p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Well, yes, right.</p>

<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">AMC Theatres=C2=A0(NYSE: AMC and APE<a class=
=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-3" style=3D"color: #000000; text-deco=
ration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #00=
0000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transparent !=
important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[3]</span>
</a>), the largest theatrical exhibitor in the United States and the world,=
 and Zoom Video Communications, Inc. (NASDAQ:ZM) today announced an innovat=
ive partnership that will turn some AMC locations throughout the United Sta=
tes into Zoom Rooms.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">As hybrid work has become more commonplace thr=
oughout the United States, Zoom Rooms at AMC will enable companies and othe=
r entities with decentralized workforces and customer bases to bring people=
 from different markets together at the same time for cohesive virtual and =
in-person events and meeting experiences. This combines the excellent exper=
ience of Zoom with the comfort and state-of-the-art sight and sound technol=
ogy of AMC=E2=80=99s modern and centrally located theatres.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">AMC and Zoom currently anticipate launching Zo=
om Rooms at AMC in up to 17 major U.S. markets sometime in 2023.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Upon launch, Zoom Rooms at AMC users will be a=
ble to easily book online, and in so doing can select their preferred theat=
res and meeting time. They will receive a three-hour block of time to virtu=
ally host their event across multiple markets at the selected theatres. AMC=
 and Zoom will provide the necessary equipment for a fully functional Zoom =
Rooms experience, in the comfort of multiple movie theatres in multiple cit=
ies simultaneously. Typical auditorium sizes are expected to range between =
75 and 150 seats, depending upon the theatre.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Additional services including food and beverag=
e offerings, possible movie viewings, and concierge-style personalized hand=
ling of meeting needs will also be available at an added cost.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The announcement is yet another innovative ini=
tiative launched by AMC designed to maximize the value creation that can co=
me from its vast amount of seating capacity.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Is this a plausible=C2=A0bet on the future of =
work? =E2=80=9CEveryone will=C2=A0work from home, but will want to gather i=
n large groups for hybrid online meetings with large groups of colleagues i=
n other cities, with popcorn=E2=80=9D? It is not really what=C2=A0<em>I=C2=
=A0</em>want, but it is not the craziest possible theory. I suppose it hedg=
es AMC=E2=80=99s=C2=A0reliance on people doing stuff (seeing movies) in per=
son, and Zoom=E2=80=99s reliance on people doing stuff (work meetings) remo=
tely. You can go to the theater in person to do a remote work meeting.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Byrne Hobart <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloo=
mbergbusiness.com/click/29633808.286504/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cudGhlZGlmZi5jby9wL3R=
oZS1iZWxvdmVkLWxvY2FsLWdyb2Nlcnktc3RvcmUtY2hhaW4/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB3=
1d58b02" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" s=
tyle=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-wid=
th: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; backgrou=
nd-color: #ccc;"> writes</a>:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The other notable thing about this=
 deal is that AMC was hurt by Covid on the revenue side, but helped on the =
financing side, since it acquired an army of dedicated retail investors. Th=
ey may have decided that their company is structurally long the pandemic an=
d its second-order effects, and have found a way for this to affect their f=
undamentals as well as their stock price.</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Another possibility is that the pandemic was, =
for quasi-mystical reasons that no one quite understands, a golden age for =
AMC, and they are doing everything they can=C2=A0to recapture that.=C2=A0If=
 you just=C2=A0<em>remind=C2=A0</em>people of the pandemic, maybe they will=
 buy AMC stock? =E2=80=9CZoom meetings,=E2=80=9D you say, and that triggers=
 a Pavlovian response where AMC=E2=80=99s stock goes up.=C2=A0It doesn=E2=
=80=99t have to make sense; it is a sort of sympathetic magic, =E2=80=9Cdo =
pandemic things and then AMC=E2=80=99s stock goes up.=E2=80=9D</p>
